coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of photoelectrochemical activity of Fe2O3 nanowires decorated with carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xin
Yang, Yunhua
Xiao, Yong-Hao
Huang, Xiaomo
Shi, Qingshan
Zhang, Wei-De
coredata.dc:description: Owing to its up-conversion photoluminescence, photo-induced electron transfer property, and excellent conductivity, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been established as effective sensitizers in combination with Fe2O3 nanowires for enhancing the catalytic activity of photoelectrochemical water oxidation. In comparison to pristine Fe2O3 nanowires, Fe2O3 nanowires decorated with CQDs demonstrate 27 orders of magnitude increase in photocurrent density at 0.23 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The mechanism of enhanced photoelectrochemical activity of CQDs/Fe2O3 composite was also investigated. Thereby, it is confirmed that the enhanced optical absorption, accelerated interfacial charge carrier transfer and effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs induced by CQDs decoration account for the enhancement of CQDs/Fe2O3 nanowire arrays in photoelectrochemical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe2O3
Nanowires
Quantum dots
Photoelectrochemical

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) UV–vis, (C) excitation-dependent PL and (D) up-conversion PL spectra of CQDs.
SEM images of (A) Fe2O3 and (B) CQDs/Fe2O3.
XRD patterns of (a) Fe2O3 and (b) CQDs/Fe2O3.
FT-IR spectra of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3.

(A) J-V curves of (a) Fe2O3, (b) 2CQDs/Fe2O3, (c) 4CQDs/Fe2O3, (d) 6CQDs/Fe2O3 and (e) 8CQDs/Fe2O3, measured in 1 M NaOH at 20 mV/s scan rate, under illumination of 100 W/cm2 (solid line is photocurrent density, dash line is measured in the dark); (B) J-t curves of (a) Fe2O3 and (b) optimum CQDs/Fe2O3 at 0.23 V with on/off cycles; (C) IPCE of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes at 0.23 V versus Ag/AgCl.
Stability curve of optimum CQDs/Fe2O3 after 20 scans.
UV–vis absorption spectra (A) and the bandgap determination (B) of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes.
EIS Nyquist plots of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes both in dark and under illumination.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms showing the capacitive currents for the Fe2O3 photoanode at five different scan rates from 0.025 to 0.3 V/s. (B) Cyclic voltammograms showing the capacitive currents for the CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanode at five different scan rates from 0.025 to 0.3 V/s. (C) The linear relationship between the capacitive current at 0.10 V versus Ag/AgCl and the scan rate for as-prepared films. The relatively electrochemically active surface areas (using the value of Fe2O3 as the baseline) are compiled in the inset.
Depiction of the energy level diagram of the CQDs/Fe2O3 and the charge separation process.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of photoelectrochemical activity of Fe2O3 nanowires decorated with carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Guangzhou, 510640, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Demay Biological Technology Co., Ltd, Guangzhou, 510663, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Demay Biological Technology Co., Ltd

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to its up-conversion photoluminescence, photo-induced electron transfer property, and excellent conductivity, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been established as effective sensitizers in combination with Fe2O3 nanowires for enhancing the catalytic activity of photoelectrochemical water oxidation. In comparison to pristine Fe2O3 nanowires, Fe2O3 nanowires decorated with CQDs demonstrate 27 orders of magnitude increase in photocurrent density at 0.23 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The mechanism of enhanced photoelectrochemical activity of CQDs/Fe2O3 composite was also investigated. Thereby, it is confirmed that the enhanced optical absorption, accelerated interfacial charge carrier transfer and effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs induced by CQDs decoration account for the enhancement of CQDs/Fe2O3 nanowire arrays in photoelectrochemical application.

A photoanode comprised of vertically aligned hematite nanowire arrays decorated with carbon quantum dots was fabricated. The CQDs/Fe2O3 electrode shows high photoelectrochemical response under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their remarkable physical and chemical properties, low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials have inspired considerable interest for decades and made great contributions to materials science. In particular, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which were accidentally discovered during the synthesis, separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [1], have become a rising star in the low-dimensional nanocarbon family in terms of their fascinating physical and chemical properties, which include high solubility in water, excellent biocompatibility, chemical inertness, nontoxicity, photo-induced electron transfer and redox properties, up- and down-converted photoluminescence, electron reservoir and so on [2–10]. Of particular importance are the quantum confinement and edge effect of CQDs as a result of small size that endow CQDs with an HOMO-LUMO gap, which suggests the light harvesting ability of CQDs as well as the possibility of electron injection from CQDs to semiconductors in contact with CQDs. In this regard, CQDs with matched HOMO-LUMO gap are expected to be combined with semiconductive nanostructures as photoanodes for remarkable photoelectrochemical oxidation. Notably, CQDs are composed of large π-conjugated structure, and accordingly are highly electrically conductive, which facilitates charge carrier separation to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency. Moreover, the appealing up- and down-converted photoluminescence of CQDs makes them a promising candidate, being capable of promoting the utilization of solar light. So far, many CQDs-based complex systems, such as TiO2/CQDs [11–14], ZnO/CQDs [15–17], WO3/CQDs [18], SnO2/CQDs [19], MoSe2/CQDs [20] and NiFe-LDH/CQDs [21], have been fabricated as photoanodes and have shown remarkable photoelectrochemical properties.

On the other hand, hematite (α-Fe2O3), which possesses such advantages as proper bandgap, excellent chemical stability, nontoxicity and abundance in earth, is a promising photoanode material that is widely used in photoelectrochemical cells [22–27]. Notably, compared to their bulk counterparts, α-Fe2O3 nanowire arrays have been demonstrated to be more efficient in photoelectrochemical water splitting in that they can promote the charge transfer and optical absorption, as well as provide a huge contact area with electrolytes [28–31]. Nevertheless, either the bulk or nanostructured photoanodes of pure α-Fe2O3 still suffers the main limitation of severe carrier recombination, which results from the combination of short hole diffusion length and poor electrical conductivity [32–35], thus preventing α-Fe2O3 from achieving a higher solar energy conversion efficiency.

Allowing for the preeminent properties of CQDs and the shortcomings of Fe2O3, surface engineering of Fe2O3 electrode with CQDs is expected to optimize photoelectrochemical activity. In this paper, we report an application of CQDs in surface engineering of Fe2O3 nanowire arrays for efficient photoelectrochemical water splitting. Compared to pristine Fe2O3, a dramatic enhancement in the photoresponse is observed over the CQDs/Fe2O3 composite. Meanwhile, the key roles of CQDs in the composite are discussed in detail.

FeCl3·H2O was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. NaNO3 was bought from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Glucose was obtained from Shanghai Bio Science & Technology Co. Ltd. NaOH was acquired from Chengdu Union Chemical Industry Reagent Institute. Deionized (DI) water employed in this work was produced using a GWA-UN pure water system (Beijing, China). Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO)-coated glass was bought from Nippon Sheet Glass Company (Japan).

Fe2O3 nanowires were synthesized by a hydrothermal procedure [36]. First, an aqueous solution containing 0.15 M FeCl3·H2O and 1 M NaNO3 was prepared. Then, 10 ml of the precursor solution were transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, where four pieces of FTO glass were put at the bottom with the conductive side facing the wall of the Teflon, and then hydrothermally heated at 95 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, the yellow β-FeOOH nanowires were fabricated on the FTO glass. The obtained β-FeOOH film was rinsed with DI water, dried naturally, and subsequently sintered at 550 °C for 2 h in air to obtain the Fe2O3 nanowires.

CQDs were prepared by an alkali-assisted ultrasonic process [18]. In detail, 9.0 g of glucose were dissolved in 50 ml DI water, while 50 ml NaOH aqueous solution (1 M) was added slowly. Subsequently, the mixing solution was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 4 h at room temperature, followed by adjusting to pH = 7 with HCl. Finally, the dark brown product was dialyzed using a semipermeable membrane (MWCO1000) to obtain CQDs aqueous solution.

The CQDs were anchored to the Fe2O3 nanowires surfaces by soaking Fe2O3 nanowires in CQDs solution at room temperature for 1 h, followed by rinsing with DI water, dried naturally, and subsequently sintered at 200 °C for 30 min under N2 to improve the structural stability. The amount of the CQDs on Fe2O3 nanowires surfaces was controlled by varying the concentration of CQDs solution. For the films reported herein, four different concentrations of CQDs solution were used, i.e., 2, 4, 6, and 8 ml raw CQDs solution diluted with 15 ml DI water, respectively. Subsequently, the obtained films were named as 2CQDs/Fe2O3, 4CQDs/Fe2O3, 6CQDs/Fe2O3 and 8CQDs/Fe2O3, respectively. For the control experiments, bare Fe2O3 was also sintered at 200 °C for 30 min under N2.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the as-prepared films was performed using an X'Pert MPD Pro X-ray diffractometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Merlin, Zeiss) was used to observe the morphologies of the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 nanostructures. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by an IR Affiniy-1 FTIR spectrometer. UV–vis spectroscopy was recorded on a Hitachi U3010 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as a reference and the fluorescence emission (PL) spectra were acquired with an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660C working station with a three-electrode cell, using platinum plate as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference, and 1.0 M NaOH aqueous solution as the electrolyte. A 500 W Xe lamp equipped with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) was chosen as the light source. Linear sweep voltammograms were recorded at 20 mV/s and the chronoamperometry curves of the films were obtained at an applied potential of 0.23 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted in an aqueous electrolyte containing K3Fe(CN)6, K4Fe(CN)6, and KCl, applying a 10 mV amplitude perturbation at frequencies between 0.01 Hz and 105 Hz. The Mott-Schottky plots were obtained at an AC frequency of 1 kHz.

In our work, CQDs were synthesized via an alkali-assisted ultrasonic treatment. Fig. 1 A presents the representative transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CQDs. As one can see from Fig. 1A, the CQDs are spherical in shape and the particle size of CQDs is around 5 nm. Fig. 1B provides the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the obtained CQDs. CQDs show strong ultraviolet (UV) absorption, with a tail extending to the visible light range. In addition, two dominant optical absorption peaks situated at around 240 and 340 nm can be observed, which may be ascribed to the π-π* transition of nanocarbon [11]. Excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of the CQDs were recorded and shown in Fig. 1C. As the excitation wavelengths change from 300 to 480 nm, the emission peak positions existing in the range from 450 to 550 nm are recorded accordingly. This phenomenon indicates that the photoinduced electrons and holes can be generated in the CQDs by optical excitation. The emission intensity increases first and then decreases, with an optimal fluorescence of 500 nm under excitation in 440 nm. Remarkably, the as-synthesized CQDs showed clear up-conversion PL properties. Fig. 1D depicts the PL emission spectra of the CQDs solution excited by longer wavelengths of light (from 600 to 800 nm) with the up-converted shorter wavelength emissions situated in the range from 450 to 500 nm. Because of such PL behaviors (both down- and up-conversion PL), the excited electrons in the CQDs can relax to ground state via fluorescence emission, which is a relatively slow process, thus beneficial for excited electrons to transfer to the Fe2O3 in contact with CQDs [12]. Hence, CQDs could act as energy conversion component to excite Fe2O3.

Fig. 2 displays the morphological features of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 films. As we can see, the Fe2O3 uniformly grown on the FTO conductive glass exhibits a nanowire-like structure with diameter of ca. 80 nm. After combined with CQDs, there emerges no significant morphology difference compared to pristine Fe2O3 nanowires.

Fig. 3 gives the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes. The XRD pattern of Fe2O3 is well consistent with the characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS 33-0664). For the XRD pattern of CQDs/Fe2O3 electrode, the main characteristic diffraction peaks are similar to those of pristine Fe2O3. However, the characteristic peak of CQDs is too weak to be observed, probably owing to the small proportion of CQDs in the composite and relatively low diffraction intensity.

The existence of the CQDs on the surface of the Fe2O3 nanowires could be validated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Fig. 4 illustrates the FTIR spectra of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3. For the bare Fe2O3 electrode, two sharp peaks at 460 and 540 cm−1 are FeO stretching and bending absorption, which are characteristic of crystalline hematite [39]. For the FTIR spectrum of CQDs/Fe2O3, three new characteristic absorption bands appeared compared with those of Fe2O3, which corresponded to the carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, indicating that the Fe2O3 nanowires were successfully modified by CQDs.

Fig. 5 A provides a set of linear sweep voltammograms (J-V) recorded on pristine Fe2O3 nanowires and Fe2O3 nanowires loaded with different amount of CQDs. The current densities of both samples measured in the dark are negligible. Even though Fe2O3 can absorb light across much of the visible spectrum, the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs resulted in a negligible photocurrent density. Loading of CQDs could obviously boost the photoelectrochemical performance of Fe2O3 nanowires. As the amount of CQDs gradually increases, the photocurrent density increases firstly and reaches the maximum number by 6CQDs/Fe2O3. Further increasing the loading of CQDs leads to a significant decay of the photocurrent density, which may be attributed to the aggregation of CQDs on nanowire surface.

The corresponding photocurrent responses (J-t) of Fe2O3 and the optimum CQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes under chopped illumination at 0.23 V vs. Ag/AgCl are presented in Fig. 5B. When the chopped light is turned on, a transient anodic photocurrent spike could be observed as the result of the transient effect in power excitation, subsequently the photocurrent density decay to achieve a steady state. In comparison to pristine Fe2O3 nanowires, the observed photocurrent density of the CQDs/Fe2O3 nanowires (94 μA/cm2) is approximately 27-fold of that of the pristine Fe2O3 nanowires (ca. 3.5 μA/cm2), indicating that incorporation of CQDs improves charge separation of CQDs/Fe2O3 and thus endows CQDs/Fe2O3 with high photoelectrochemical performance. To investigate the photoactivity of the photocatalysts as a function of wavelength, the incident photon to electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were conducted on the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes at 0.23 V versus Ag/AgCl. As indicated in Fig. 7 C, the CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanode shows a 15-fold improvement over the Fe2O3 photoanode at 420 nm at an applied potential of 0.23 V, which can be ascribed to the change in optical absorption by incorporating CQDs.

In addition to the high photocurrent, the stability of the photoelectrode is also a key factor for an efficient photoelectrochemical water splitting system. Fig. 6 shows that the photocurrent response of CQDs/Fe2O3 after 20 cycles is similar to that of the first scan, demonstrating the good durability of CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanode.

To elucidate the roles of CQDs, we investigated the optical properties of the pristine Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 as optical property is a critical factor that influences photoelectrochemical performance. Fig. 7 presents the UV–vis absorption spectra of both Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes. As we can see in Fig. 7B, both Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 samples display similar absorption edge, indicating coating with CQDs does not change the band gap of Fe2O3. The incorporation of CQDs enables improved optical absorption of Fe2O3, implying that CQDs/Fe2O3 composite may produce more photogenerated electrons and holes than pristine Fe2O3 whereas CQDs exhibit a crucial influence on utilizing sunlight [37,38].

In order to further investigate the photoinduced carrier separation mechanism, the energy band structures of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 were determined by Mott−Schottky plots and valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS). Both Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes exhibited positive slope, as being expected for an n-type semiconductor. According to the Mott–Schottky plots (Figure S2A), the flat-band potentials (Vfb) of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 were determined to be −0.17 and −0.40 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl electrode), corresponding to 0.03 and −0.20 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. It was reported that the flat-band potential is approximately equivalent to the Fermi level (Ef) for n-type semiconductors. Hence, the Fermi levels (Ef) of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 were 0.03 and −0.20 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. The VB-XPS spectra (Figure S2B) revealed that the energy gaps between the valence band (VB) and Ef were 1.70 and 1.50 eV for the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3, respectively. Therefore, the VB positions for the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 could be calculated to be 1.73 and 1.30 eV, respectively. Simultaneously, the CB positions of the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 were calculated, based on the Eg of −0.29 and −0.72 eV (CB = VB-Eg), respectively. The energy band alignments of the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 are schematically illustrated in Figure S2C.

The room temperature PL spectra of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 samples between 445 and 480 nm excited by light with wavelength of 350 nm are shown in Figure S3. As it is well known, the lower intensity in PL means the higher separation and transfer efficiency of electron-hole pairs. Obviously, the CQDs on the surface of Fe2O3 facilitate the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and the transfer to external circuit [41].

The electrode/electrolyte interfacial properties were investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 8 shows the EIS Nyquist plots of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 electrodes both in dark and under illumination. The semicircles at high frequency reflect the interfacial charge-transfer resistances. A larger semicircle represents larger interfacial charge-transfer resistance and lower charge separation efficiency [39,40]. Under illumination, the charge-transfer resistances of both Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 decrease considerably on account of the generation of abundant electrons and holes induced by illumination [41]. In the meantime, the semicircle of the CQDs/Fe2O3 is smaller than that of Fe2O3 either under illumination or in dark, which indicates a faster interfacial charge transfer due to the excellent charge transport properties of CQDs. Therefore, CQDs in CQDs/Fe2O3 electrode can not only serve as a sensitizer to enhance the light harvesting ability of the Fe2O3, but also accelerate interfacial charge transfer, so a higher photoelectrochemical activity in the CQDs/Fe2O3 electrode was achieved.

Fig. 9 A and B shows the capacitive currents of the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes with different scan rates, with OCP of 0.10 V versus Ag/AgCl. The non-Faradaic potential region could be defined from 0.05 to 0.15 V versus Ag/AgCl [43]. Assuming that the intrinsic specific capacitance was the same for all photoanodes, the ECSA could be determined by the ratio of the capacitive current, which is shown in Fig. 9C. Upon introduction of CQDs, the double-layer capacitance of the film was widened, and more active sites for the surface reaction were created. Furthermore, the larger ECSA would result in reduction of the charge-transfer resistance and smaller interface resistance, which agree with the EIS result.

Based on the above experimental results, it could be expected that CQDs play three important roles in enhancing the photoelectrochemical activity of Fe2O3. The schematic diagram (Fig. 10 ) illustrates the energy band structure and the principle of the separation of charge carriers in CQDs/Fe2O3. First, the quantum confinement and edge effect of the as-synthesized CQDs may endow them with HOMO-LUMO gaps. Electrons in the CQDs can be excited by photons with adequate energy and subsequently transfer to the LUMO of the CQDs from the HOMO. As the LUMO of the CQDs is more negative than the conduction band (CB) of Fe2O3, the excited electrons can migrate from the LUMO of the CQDs to the CB of the Fe2O3, and ultimately transport along the Fe2O3 nanowires axial direction to the counter electrode for the photoelectrochemical water splitting process. The rest of the photogenerated holes can be injected to the electrolyte solution for water oxidation, and hence decreasing the probability of charge recombination. Secondly, CQDs can absorb longer wavelengths of light and convert into shorter wavelengths of light as a result of up-conversion PL property, which subsequently promote Fe2O3 to produce electron-hole pairs and thus enhance the photoelectrochemical activity. Thirdly, due to the super charge transport property of CQDs, electrons could be shuttled rapidly between the CQDs and Fe2O3 interface, so the possibility of charge recombination is decreased [42]. Moreover, interfacial charge transfer resistance reduces dramatically, resulting from CODs' excellent charge transport property. All these factors working together are responsible for the enhanced photoelectrochemical activity of CQDs/Fe2O3 composite compared to the pristine Fe2O3.

In summary, Fe2O3 nanowire arrays decorated with CQDs were successfully synthesized as an efficient photoanode for photoelectrochemical water splitting. Satisfactorily, the optimized CQDs/Fe2O3 electrode exhibited superior photocurrent density to pristine Fe2O3, which is approximately 27-fold of that of the pristine Fe2O3 at 0.23 V. Herein, CQDs played vital roles in promoting the photoelectrochemical activity of the Fe2O3 photoanode. First, the up-conversion PL property of CQDs facilitated the optical absorption of hybrid CQDs/Fe2O3 electrode. Meanwhile, the photo-induced electron transfer property and excellent conductivity of CQDs can effectively suppress recombination of the photogenerated electrons and holes, and accelerate the interfacial charge transfer, thereby leading to significantly enhanced photoelectrochemical conversion efficiency. The work demonstrates the significant potential of CQDs as a promising material to enhance the activity of semiconductor photoanodes for photoelectrochemical solar energy conversion applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (2014A030311039) and the Pearl River S & T Nova Program of Guangzhou (201610010032).
